title: 5  Internal Convection
text:
5.5 Temperature Variation Along the Pipe
How much pipe needs to be heated in order to reach a given operating temperature? Now that we have \(h\), we can answer this question.

5.5.1 Known surface temperature \(T_s\)


5.5.2 1. \(T_s = \text{constant}\)
Write an energy balance relating thermodynamics and heat transfer
\[
d\dot{Q} = \dot{m}\,c_p\,dT_m = h\cdot P\cdot dx\cdot(T_s - T_m)
\]
where \(P\) is the perimeter. Rearranging and integrating
\[
\int\frac{dT_m}{T_s - T_m} = \int\frac{hP}{\dot{m}c_p}\,dx
\]
\[
-\ln(T_s - T_m)\bigg|_{T_{m,i}}^{T_m(x)} = \frac{hP}{\dot{m}c_p}\,x
\]
\[
\ln\left[\frac{T_s - T_m(x)}{T_s - T_{m,i}}\right] = -\frac{hP}{\dot{m}c_p}\,x
\]
we are left with
\[
\frac{T_s - T_m(x)}{T_s - T_{m,i}} = \exp\!\left(\frac{-hP}{\dot{m}c_p}\,x\right).
\]


5.5.3 Known heat flux \(q''_s\)
Again, write an energy balance:
\[
\dot{m}\,c_p\,dT_m = q_s''\cdot P\cdot dx.
\]
Rearrange and integrate
\[
\int dT_m = \int\frac{q_s''\cdot P}{\dot{m}\,c_p}\,dx
\]
leaving
\[
T_m(x) = \frac{q_s''\,P}{\dot{m}\,c_p}\,x + T_{m,i}.
\]
Notice that this does not require \(h\)!

title: Composite Plane Walls – Interactive Demo
text:
From \(R\) to \(R''\)
Recall the thermal resistance analogy. For a single planar wall of area \(A\), thickness \(L\), and conductivity \(k\):
\[R_\text{cond} = \frac{L}{kA} \qquad [\text{K/W}]\]
so that \(\dot{Q} = \Delta T / R_\text{cond}\). It is convenient to factor out the area and work with the thermal resistance per unit area:
\[R''_\text{cond} = \frac{L}{k} \qquad [\text{m}^2\text{·K/W}]\]
The two are related by \(R = R''/A\). Working in \(R''\) keeps the formula independent of wall size – you characterize the wall geometry and materials once, then scale to any area at the end.
For a wall with convection on both sides and three conduction layers in series, all resistances add:
\[R''_\text{tot} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{h_L}}_{\text{left conv}} + \underbrace{\frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3}}_{\text{conduction}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{h_R}}_{\text{right conv}}\]
The steady heat flux through the wall is
\[q'' = \frac{T_{\infty,L} - T_{\infty,R}}{R''_\text{tot}}\]
and the total heat transfer rate for area \(A\) is \(\dot{Q} = q'' \cdot A\).

title: Composite Plane Walls – Interactive Demo
Composite Plane Walls – Interactive Demo
Composite Plane Walls – Interactive Demo
text:
What to look for
Observation 1 – Steepest slope = highest resistance. The temperature drops most steeply across the layer with the largest \(L/k\). Try setting one layer to \(k = 0.04\) W/m·K (typical insulation) and watch nearly all the temperature drop concentrate there.
Observation 2 – Convection resistance can dominate. Reduce \(h_L\) to 5 W/m²·K (still-air natural convection). You will likely find \(1/h_L\) is the biggest resistor (depends on the other parameters) – a reminder that adding more insulation has diminishing returns when convection is the bottleneck.
Observation 3 – Series resistance is additive. Double any single layer thickness and \(R''_\text{tot}\) increases by exactly \(\Delta L / k\) – visible immediately in the resistance network below the plot.
Observation 4 – Fourier’s Law relates the slopes. Since this is a steady system, we have that the heat flux throughout is constant. That is, within the first and second layers, we’ll have
\[
-k_1 \frac{dT}{dx} = -k_2 \frac{dT}{dx}
\]
If \(k_1 > k_2\), which layer is going to have the larger slope magnitude?

title: Fin Temperature Profile — Interactive Demo
text:
Setup
We are looking at a single fin of uniform cross-section, attached to a hot surface at \(x = 0\) and with an adiabatic tip at \(x = L\). The temperature profile along the fin is
\[
T(x) = T_\infty + (T_b - T_\infty)\frac{\cosh\!\left(m(1 - x/L)\right)}{\cosh(m)}
\]
where
\[
m = \sqrt{\frac{hPL^2}{kA_c}}.
\]

title: Fin Temperature Profile — Interactive Demo
text:
Warning



I keep repeating the definition of \(m\) because there are different versions of it! This is the one we used in the video.

title: Fin Temperature Profile — Interactive Demo
Fin Temperature Profile — Interactive Demo
text:
What to look for
The shape is controlled by one number. Take note of how the plot looks in a given situation, then increase \(L\) by a factor of 2 and decrease \(h\) by a factor of 4. You should get the same curve. The curve’s shape depends only on \(m = \sqrt{hPL^2/kA_c}\). Instead of thinking about 7 parameters (when you include \(T_b\) and \(T_\infty\) as well), the non-dimensionalization process reveals we can think about 1 parameter.
Large \(m\) means the fin “runs out of steam” early. Increase \(h\) or \(L\), or decrease \(k\), such that \(m\) gets larger. You’ll see that the temperature plummets toward \(T_\infty\) more and more abruptly, and \(\eta_f\) falls-off too.
The adiabatic-tip condition is visible. No matter how extreme the parameters, the curve is always horizontal at \(x = L\). This is baked-in to the math: we used this as one of our boundary conditions (in this solution).
Conductivity and convection are adversaries. There is a competition occurring in fins: conduction is trying to carry heat through the fin, convection is trying to take heat out of the fin. In the case where \(k\) is high relative to \(h\), the fin can be nearly isothermal at \(T_b\). If \(h\) is high relative to \(k\), convection pulls heat out of the fin before it has a chance to conduct down the length, and much of the fin will be at nearly \(T_\infty\). This competition is characterized by \(m\).


We can also think of this as teamwork: we want the heat to readily conduct through the fin so that the temperature is high everywhere within the fin because that means we will get the largest amount of convective heat transfer out of the fin.
Efficiency and tip temperature are two ways to say the same thing. If \(T(L)\) is close to \(T_\infty\), \(\eta_f\) is low. If \(T(L) \approx T_b\), the fin is nearly isothermal and \(\eta_f \approx 1\).

title: Sphere Temperature Profile — Interactive Demo
Sphere Temperature Profile — Interactive Demo
text:
What to look for
The Lumped Capacitance Model breaks down at large Bi. When \(\text{Bi} \ll 1\) all three curves nearly coincide: the sphere equilibrates internally much faster than it exchanges heat with the fluid. As \(\text{Bi}\) increases the surface cools faster than the core, and the lumped model — which predicts no spatial gradient — diverges from the correct solution.
The One-Term Approximation accuracy improves with Fo. At small \(\text{Fo}\) (say 0.05) many Fourier terms contribute because the \(n\geq 2\) exponentials \(\exp({-\zeta_n^2 \text{Fo}})\) have not yet decayed. Drag \(\text{Fo}\) above 0.2 and watch the one-term curve collapse onto the full series.
Center vs. surface temperature. The center (\(r = 0\)) always lags behind the surface (\(r/R = 1\)). The larger the Biot number, the steeper the radial gradient.

The initial temperature is \(T_i = 800\ \text{°C}\). Find the center and surface temperatures after 1 minute.
\(\text{Bi} = hR/k = 500 \times 0.020 / 50 = 0.200\); \(\quad \text{Fo} = \alpha t / R^2 = (1.4\times10^{-5})(60)/(0.020)^2 = 2.10\)

title: Semi-Infinite Solid — Interactive Demo
Semi-Infinite Solid — Interactive Demo
text:
What to look for
Case I — error function profile, surface pinned at Tₛ. The surface is always exactly \(T_s\) and far away the temperature is still \(T_i\).
Case II — surface temperature grows without bound. There is no target surface temperature; instead energy arrives at a constant rate \(q_0''\). The surface temperature grows \(\sim\!\sqrt{t}\), which is physically unrealistic at long times. Notice that deeper in the solid the profiles look qualitatively similar to Case I.
Case III — convection slows the approach. The surface doesn’t immediately reach \(T_\infty\)… it approaches it gradually. A small \(h\) means the convective resistance dominates: little heat is transferred and the solid barely warms. A large \(h\) drives Case III toward Case I — the surface is heated so efficiently that it behaves like a prescribed temperature.

title: Semi-Infinite Solid — Interactive Demo
section: Worked example — Case I thaw depth
text:
Worked example — Case I thaw depth
Frozen soil (\(\alpha = 5 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}\), \(T_i = -5\,^\circ\text{C}\)) has its surface suddenly raised to \(T_s = 15\,^\circ\text{C}\). How deep does the 0°C thaw front reach after 90 days?
We need to determine the location where \(T = 0\,^\circ\text{C}\)$, so we solve for \(\eta\) such that \(\text{erf}(\eta) = T-T_s/(T_i - T_s)=0.75\). Can ask Wolfram Alpha, and will determine \(\eta=0.8134\). Then the thaw depth is
\[
x = 2\sqrt{\alpha t}\cdot\eta = 2\sqrt{(5\times10^{-7}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s})\,(90\ \text{days}\times 86{,}400\ \text{s/day})}\times 0.8134 \approx 3.21\ \text{m}.
\]
Verify this against the “90 days” curve in Case I above (with \(T_s = 15\,^\circ\text{C}\), \(T_i = -5\,^\circ\text{C}\)): the curve should cross the dashed \(T=0\) line at about \(x = 3.2\ \text{m}\).

title: Flat Plate — Interactive Demo
Flat Plate — Interactive Demo
text:
What to look for
Notice the axis units. The \(x\)-axis is in meters, but the \(y\)-axis in the boundary layer plot is in millimeters — boundary layers are very thin! A plate a meter long might have a laminar BL only a few millimeters thick at its trailing edge.
h drops through the laminar region — as \(x^{-1/2}\). The boundary layer thickens away from the leading edge, the temperature gradient at the wall decreases, and so does the local heat flux for a fixed \(\Delta T\). This is counterintuitive at first: moving further along the plate makes heat transfer worse, locally.
h rises sharply through the transition zone. As the boundary layer goes turbulent near \(x_c = \text{Re}_c \cdot \nu / U_\infty\), turbulent mixing transports heat perpendicular to the plate far more effectively, and \(h\) rises rapidly. (Drag increases too — the same mixing that improves heat transfer also raises skin friction.) The smooth S-curve in the demo is schematic: real transition width depends on freestream turbulence intensity, surface roughness, and pressure gradient — it can be very sharp in a clean wind tunnel or spread over 30–40% of \(x_c\) in a noisy environment.
h drops more slowly in the turbulent region — as \(x^{-1/5}\). The turbulent BL still thickens, but the radial mixing partially compensates.
Things to try: - Increase \(U_\infty\) until the transition marker appears — watch \(\bar{h}\) jump as turbulence takes over more of the plate. - Switch to Water — \(\bar{h}\) increases dramatically. Water’s high \(k\) and \(c_p\) make it a far more effective convective coolant. - Try Mercury — \(\text{Pr} \approx 0.025\), so the thermal boundary layer is much thicker than the velocity BL. The \(h\) values are very high (high \(k_f\)) but the Pr scaling pulls them back. A genuinely different regime. - Adjust individual properties after snapping a preset — e.g., what if you kept air’s \(\nu\) but gave it water’s \(k\)?

title: Flat Plate — Interactive Demo
text:
Worked example — mixed boundary layer
Air at \(T_\infty = 25\,^\circ\text{C}\), \(U_\infty = 10\) m/s flows over a plate \(L = 2\) m long, \(w = 1\) m wide, held at \(T_s = 75\,^\circ\text{C}\). Find the total heat transfer rate \(\dot{Q}\).
Properties (25°C, matching the demo defaults): \[k_f = 0.02551\;\text{W/m·K}, \quad \nu = 1.562\times10^{-5}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}, \quad \text{Pr} = 0.730\]
Step 1 — Set up the integral.
The local heat flux at position \(x\) is \(q''(x) = h(x)(T_s - T_\infty)\), so the total heat transfer rate is
\[\dot{Q} = w\int_0^L q''(x)\,dx = w(T_s-T_\infty)\int_0^L h(x)\,dx.\]
Since the surface temperature is uniform, \((T_s - T_\infty)\) comes outside the integral — but \(h(x)\) does not, because it varies with position. We can define the average coefficient as
\[\bar{h} \equiv \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L h(x)\,dx \qquad \Rightarrow \qquad \dot{Q} = \bar{h}\,wL\,(T_s-T_\infty).\]
So finding \(\dot{Q}\) reduces to evaluating that integral.
Step 2 — Check for transition; split the integrand.
\[\text{Re}_L = \frac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{10\times2}{1.562\times10^{-5}} = 1.28\times10^6 > \text{Re}_c = 5\times10^5 \quad \checkmark\;\text{mixed}\]
Transition occurs at \(x_c = \text{Re}_c\,\nu/U_\infty = 0.78\) m, so the integral splits:
\[\int_0^L h(x)\,dx = \underbrace{\int_0^{x_c} h_\text{lam}(x)\,dx}_{\text{laminar portion}} + \underbrace{\int_{x_c}^L h_\text{turb}(x)\,dx}_{\text{turbulent portion}}\]
Step 3 — Write \(h(x)\) explicitly in each region.
Unpacking \(h(x) = \text{Nu}_x \cdot k_f/x\) with the local correlations:
\[h_\text{lam}(x) = \frac{k_f}{x}\cdot 0.332\,\text{Re}_x^{1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3} = 0.332\,k_f\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}\!\left(\frac{U_\infty}{\nu}\right)^{\!1/2} x^{-1/2}\]
\[h_\text{turb}(x) = \frac{k_f}{x}\cdot 0.0296\,\text{Re}_x^{4/5}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3} = 0.0296\,k_f\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}\!\left(\frac{U_\infty}{\nu}\right)^{\!4/5} x^{-1/5}\]
The integrands are power laws — \(x^{-1/2}\) in the laminar zone and \(x^{-1/5}\) in the turbulent zone — which is exactly the \(h(x)\) shape plotted in the demo.
Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals.
\[\int_0^{x_c}\! h_\text{lam}\,dx = 0.332\,k_f\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}\!\left(\frac{U_\infty}{\nu}\right)^{\!1/2}\!\int_0^{x_c} x^{-1/2}\,dx = 0.332\,k_f\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}\!\left(\frac{U_\infty}{\nu}\right)^{\!1/2}\cdot 2x_c^{1/2}\]
\[= 0.664\,k_f\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}\,\text{Re}_{x_c}^{1/2}\]
\[\int_{x_c}^{L}\! h_\text{turb}\,dx = 0.0296\,k_f\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}\!\left(\frac{U_\infty}{\nu}\right)^{\!4/5}\!\int_{x_c}^{L} x^{-1/5}\,dx = 0.0296\,k_f\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}\!\left(\frac{U_\infty}{\nu}\right)^{\!4/5}\cdot\frac{5}{4}\!\left(L^{4/5}-x_c^{4/5}\right)\]
\[= 0.037\,k_f\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}\!\left(\text{Re}_L^{4/5} - \text{Re}_{x_c}^{4/5}\right)\]
Step 5 — Combine and compute \(\bar{h}\).
\[\bar{h} = \frac{k_f\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}}{L}\!\left[0.664\,\text{Re}_{x_c}^{1/2} + 0.037\!\left(\text{Re}_L^{4/5} - \text{Re}_{x_c}^{4/5}\right)\right]\]
\[= \frac{0.02551\times0.900}{2}\!\left[\underbrace{0.664\times707}_{469} + 0.037\underbrace{\left(76{,}850 - 36{,}200\right)}_{40{,}650}\right] = 0.01148\times\left[469+1504\right]\]
\[\boxed{\bar{h} \approx 22.6\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}}\]
Step 6 — Total heat transfer rate.
\[\dot{Q} = \bar{h}\,wL\,(T_s-T_\infty) = 22.6\times(1\times2)\times(75-25) = \boxed{2260\;\text{W}}\]
Set \(U_\infty = 10\), \(L = 2\) in the demo (Air preset) and confirm the average convection coefficient reads approximately 22.6. Now compare to a fully laminar plate: set \(U_\infty = 3\) m/s (\(\text{Re}_L = 3.84\times10^5 < 5\times10^5\), no transition). The laminar-only \(\bar{h}\) drops to roughly 8 W/m²·K — for the same \(\Delta T\) and plate area that would reduce the heat transfer rate from 2.26 kW to only 0.8 kW.

title: Log-Mean Temperature Difference — Interactive Demo
Log-Mean Temperature Difference — Interactive Demo
text:
What to look for
The tube bank: constant \(T_s\). The default view shows exactly the setup from the chapter — the tube surface is at constant \(T_s\) (flat red line), and the external flow heats up exponentially from \(T_\text{in}\) to \(T_\text{out}\). Try setting \(T_s = 70\,^\circ\)C, \(T_\text{in} = 15\,^\circ\)C, \(T_\text{out} = 41\,^\circ\)C to match the worked example below and confirm \(\Delta T_{lm} \approx 40.6\,^\circ\)C.






Note



A constant-temperature surface can occur during a phase change, and it also approximates any stream with a very large heat capacity rate \(C = \dot{m}c_p\): if enough fluid is flowing, its temperature barely shifts and it looks flat on the plot.


Arithmetic mean always overestimates. The summary panel shows the arithmetic mean alongside \(\Delta T_{lm}\). The arithmetic mean is always greater than or equal to \(\Delta T_{lm}\). Using it in \(\dot{Q} = \overline{h}A\,\Delta T\) would overestimate the heat transfer rate. The correction matters most when \(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2\) is far from 1.
Counter flow outperforms parallel flow — with the same terminal temperatures. (Coming in a few weeks, but worth a peek now.) Switch to “Counter flow” or “Parallel flow” with \(T_h\): 90 → 60 °C, \(T_c\): 20 → 55 °C. The arithmetic mean \(\Delta T\) is the same for both — it only depends on the four terminal values. But \(\Delta T_{lm}\) is notably higher for counter flow, because counter flow keeps \(\Delta T_1\) and \(\Delta T_2\) more balanced; the log-mean penalizes large imbalance. That gap is the engineering motivation for counter-flow designs.
When \(\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2\), \(\Delta T_{lm} = \Delta T_1\) exactly. (To show this, remember L’Hôpital’s Rule.) The profiles become straight lines and the arithmetic mean is correct. This happens in counter flow when the two streams have equal capacity rates — a balanced, perfectly efficient exchanger.

title: Log-Mean Temperature Difference — Interactive Demo
section: Worked example — tube bank as isothermal hot surface
text:
Worked example — tube bank as isothermal hot surface
A bank of \(N_T = 4\) tubes wide and \(N_L = 20\) rows deep (\(N = 80\) total) is arranged in an aligned square pitch: \(D = 0.025\) m, \(S_T = S_L = 0.050\) m, tube length \(L = 1\) m. Air approaches at \(V_\infty = 4\) m/s and \(T_\text{in} = 15\,^\circ\)C; the tubes are held at \(T_s = 70\,^\circ\)C. Find \(\overline{h}\), the outlet temperature, \(\Delta T_{lm}\), and the total heat transfer.
Step 1 — Find \(\overline{h}\) from the tube bank correlation.
We need properties at \(T_\text{avg} = (T_\text{in} + T_\text{out})/2\), but we don’t know \(T_\text{out}\) yet. We’ll guess \(T_\text{out} \approx 40\,^\circ\)C, putting \(T_\text{avg} \approx 27\,^\circ\)C \(\approx 300\) K, and check afterward. Air properties at 300 K (from Wolfram Alpha): \(k = 0.0264\;\text{W/m·K}\), \(\nu = 15.7\times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^2\text{/s}\), \(\rho = 1.18\;\text{kg/m}^3\), \(c_p = 1005\;\text{J/kg·K}\), \(\text{Pr} = 0.707\). At \(T_s = 70\,^\circ\)C: \(\text{Pr}_s = 0.702\).
Maximum velocity through the narrowest cross-section between tubes:
\[V_\text{max} = \frac{S_T}{S_T - D}\,V_\infty = \frac{0.050}{0.050 - 0.025}\times 4 = 8\;\text{m/s}\]
\[\text{Re}_{D,\max} = \frac{V_\text{max}\,D}{\nu} = \frac{8\times 0.025}{15.7\times10^{-6}} = 12{,}700\]
This falls in the range \(10^3\)–\(2\times10^5\) for an aligned bank with \(S_T/S_L = 1.0 > 0.7\), so from the table: \(C_1 = 0.27\), \(m = 0.63\). Since \(N_L = 20\), no correction factor is needed (\(C_2 = 1\)). The Prandtl ratio \((0.707/0.702)^{1/4} \approx 1.00\) — essentially no effect for air — so:
\[\overline{\text{Nu}}_D = 0.27\times 12700^{0.63}\times 0.707^{0.36} = 0.27\times 385\times 0.883 \approx 92.0\]
\[\overline{h} = \frac{k}{D}\,\overline{\text{Nu}}_D = \frac{0.0264}{0.025}\times 92.0 \approx 97\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}\]
Step 2 — Estimate the outlet temperature.
\[\frac{T_s - T_\text{out}}{T_s - T_\text{in}} = \exp\!\left(\frac{-\pi D N\,\overline{h}}{\rho\,V_\infty\,N_T\,S_T\,c_p}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{-\pi\times 0.025\times 80\times 97}{1.18\times 4\times 4\times 0.050\times 1005}\right) = \exp(-0.642) = 0.526\]
\[T_\text{out} = 70 - 0.526\times(70-15) = 70 - 28.9 \approx 41\,^\circ\text{C}\]
\(T_\text{avg} = (15 + 41)/2 = 28\,^\circ\text{C} \approx 300\) K — consistent with our property choice. ✓
Step 3 — Apply the log-mean formula.
\[\Delta T_1 = T_s - T_\text{in} = 70 - 15 = 55\,^\circ\text{C}, \qquad \Delta T_2 = T_s - T_\text{out} = 70 - 41 = 29\,^\circ\text{C}\]
\[\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{55 - 29}{\ln(55/29)} = \frac{26}{\ln 1.897} = \frac{26}{0.640} = 40.6\,^\circ\text{C}\]
Check against the demo: in the default “Constant \(T_s\)” mode, set \(T_s = 70\,^\circ\)C, \(T_\text{in} = 15\,^\circ\)C, \(T_\text{out} = 41\,^\circ\)C. The summary should read \(\Delta T_{lm} \approx 40.6\,^\circ\)C.
The arithmetic mean gives \((55 + 29)/2 = 42\,^\circ\)C — about 3% above \(\Delta T_{lm}\) here. Not catastrophic, but the error grows quickly as \(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2\) moves away from 1.
Step 4 — Total heat transfer:
\[\dot{Q} = N\,\overline{h}\,\pi D L\,\Delta T_{lm} = 80\times 97\times\pi\times 0.025\times 1\times 40.6 \approx 24.7\;\text{kW}\]
